sections.label: Section 1277
sections.original: σὺ δὲ νὴ τὸν Ἑρμῆν προσκεκλήσεσθαί γέ μοι ,
sections.translation: And you appear to me , by Hermes , to be going to be summoned , if you will not pay me the money ?

sections.label: Section 1278b
sections.original: κάτειπέ νυν ,
sections.translation: Tell me now , whether you think that Jupiter

sections.label: Section 1280
sections.original: ὕειν ὕδωρ ἑκάστοτʼ , ἢ τὸν ἥλιον
sections.translation: always rains fresh rain on each occasion , or that the sun draws from below the same water back again ?

sections.label: Section 1282
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ ἔγωγʼ ὁπότερον , οὐδέ μοι μέλει .
sections.translation: I know not which ; nor do I care .

sections.label: Section 1283
sections.original: πῶς οὖν ἀπολαβεῖν τἀργύριον δίκαιος εἶ ,
sections.translation: How then is it just that you should recover your money , if you know nothing of meteorological matters ?

sections.label: Section 1285
sections.original: ἀλλʼ εἰ σπανίζεις , τἀργυρίου μοι τὸν τόκον
sections.translation: Well , if you are in want , pay me the interest of my money .

sections.label: Section 1286b
sections.original: τοῦτο δʼ ἔσθʼ ὁ τόκος τί θηρίον ;
sections.translation: What sort of animal is this interest ?

sections.label: Section 1287
sections.original: τί δʼ ἄλλο γʼ ἢ κατὰ μῆνα καὶ καθʼ ἡμέραν
sections.translation: Most assuredly the money is always becoming more and more every month and every day as the time slips away .

sections.label: Section 1289b
sections.original: καλῶς λέγεις .
sections.translation: You say well . What then ? Is it possible that you consider the sea to be greater now than formerly ?

sections.label: Section 1291b
sections.original: μὰ Δίʼ ἀλλʼ ἴσην .
sections.translation: No , by Jupiter , but equal ; for it is not fitting that it should be greater .

sections.label: Section 1292b
sections.original: κᾆτα πῶς
sections.translation: And how then , you wretch does this become no way greater , though the rivers flow into it , while you

sections.label: Section 1295
sections.original: ζητεῖς ποιῆσαι τἀργύριον πλεῖον τὸ σόν ;
sections.translation: seek to increase your money ? Will you not take yourself off from my house ? Bring me the goad .

sections.label: Section 1297b
sections.original: ταῦτʼ ἐγὼ μαρτύρομαι .
sections.translation: I call you to witness these things .

sections.label: Section 1298
sections.original: ὕπαγε . τί μέλλεις ; οὐκ ἐλᾷς ὦ σαμφόρα ;
sections.translation: Go ! Why do you delay ? Won ’ t you march , Mr . Blood-horse ?

sections.label: Section 1299
sections.original: ταῦτʼ οὐχ ὕβρις δῆτʼ ἐστίν ;
sections.translation: Is not this an insult , pray ?

sections.label: Section 1299b
sections.original: ᾄξεις ; ἐπιαλῶ
sections.translation: Will you move quickly ? Pricks him behind with the goad . I ’ ll lay on you , goading you behind , you outrigger ? Do you fly ? Amynias runs off . I thought I should stir you , together with your wheels and your two-horse chariots .

sections.label: Section 1303
sections.original: οἷον τὸ πραγμάτων ἐρᾶν φλαύρων · ὁ γὰρ
sections.translation: What a thing it is to love evil courses ! For this old man , having loved them ,

sections.label: Section 1305
sections.original: ἀποστερῆσαι βούλεται
sections.translation: wishes to withhold the money that he borrowed . And he will certainly meet with something today , which will perhaps cause this sophist

sections.label: Section 1310
sections.original: ἀνθʼ ὧν πανουργεῖν ἤρξατʼ , ἐξαίφνης λαβεῖν κακόν τι .
sections.translation: to suddenly receive some misfortune , in return for the knaveries he has begun .

sections.label: Section 1311
sections.original: οἶμαι γὰρ αὐτὸν αὐτίχʼ εὑρήσειν ὅπερ
sections.translation: For I think that he will presently find what has been long boiling up , that his son is skilful to speak opinions

sections.label: Section 1315
sections.original: τοῖσιν δικαίοις , ὥστε νικᾶν
sections.translation: opposed to justice , so as to overcome all with whomsoever he holds converse , even if he advance most villainous doctrines ;

sections.label: Section 1320
sections.original: ἴσως δʼ ἴσως βουλήσεται κἄφωνον αὐτὸν εἶναι .
sections.translation: and perhaps , perhaps his father will wish that he were even speechless .

sections.label: Section 1321
sections.original: ἰοὺ ἰού .
sections.translation: ( running out of the house pursued by his son ). Hollo ! Hollo ! O neighbours , and kinsfolk , and fellow-tribesmen , defend me , by all means , who am being beaten ! Ah me , unhappy man , for my head and jaw ! Wretch ! Do you beat your father ?

sections.label: Section 1325b
sections.original: φήμʼ ὦ πάτερ .
sections.translation: Yes , father .

sections.label: Section 1326
sections.original: ὁρᾶθʼ ὁμολογοῦνθʼ ὅτι με τύπτει .
sections.translation: You see him owning that he beats me .

sections.label: Section 1326b
sections.original: καὶ μάλα .
sections.translation: Certainly .

sections.label: Section 1327
sections.original: ὦ μιαρὲ καὶ πατραλοῖα καὶ τοιχωρύχε .
sections.translation: O wretch , and parricide , and house-breaker !

sections.label: Section 1328
sections.original: αὖθίς με ταὐτὰ ταῦτα καὶ πλείω λέγε .
sections.translation: Say the same things of me again , and more . Do you know that I take pleasure in being much abused ?

sections.label: Section 1330
sections.original: ὦ λακκόπρωκτε .
sections.translation: You blackguard !

sections.label: Section 1330b
sections.original: πάττε πολλοῖς τοῖς ῥόδοις .
sections.translation: Sprinkle me with roses in abundance .

sections.label: Section 1331
sections.original: τὸν πατέρα τύπτεις ;
sections.translation: Do you beat your father ?

sections.label: Section 1331b
sections.original: κἀποφανῶ γε νὴ Δία
sections.translation: And will prove too , by Jupiter ! that I beat you with justice .

sections.label: Section 1332b
sections.original: ὦ μιαρώτατε ,
sections.translation: O thou most rascally ! Why , how can it be just to beat a father ?

sections.label: Section 1334
sections.original: ἔγωγʼ ἀποδείξω καί σε νικήσω λέγων .
sections.translation: I will demonstrate it , and will overcome you in argument .

sections.label: Section 1335
sections.original: τουτὶ σὺ νικήσεις ;
sections.translation: Will you overcome me in this ?

sections.label: Section 1335b
sections.original: πολύ γε καὶ ῥᾳδίως .
sections.translation: Yea , by much and easily . But choose which of the two Causes you wish to speak .

sections.label: Section 1337
sections.original: ποίοιν λόγοιν ;
sections.translation: Of what two Causes ?

sections.label: Section 1337b
sections.original: τὸν κρείττονʼ ἢ τὸν ἥττονα .
sections.translation: The better , or the worse ?

sections.label: Section 1338
sections.original: ἐδιδαξάμην μέντοι σε νὴ Δίʼ ὦ μέλε
sections.translation: Marry , I did get you taught to speak against justice , by Jupiter , my friend , if you are

sections.label: Section 1340
sections.original: μέλλεις ἀναπείσειν , ὡς δίκαιον καὶ καλὸν
sections.translation: going to persuade me of this , that it is just and honourable for a father to be beaten by his sons !

sections.label: Section 1342
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οἴομαι μέντοι σʼ ἀναπείσειν , ὥστε γε
sections.translation: I think I shall certainly persuade you ; so that , when you have heard , not even you yourself will say anything against it .

sections.label: Section 1344
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅ τι καὶ λέξεις ἀκοῦσαι βούλομαι .
sections.translation: Well , now , I am willing to hear what you have to say .

sections.label: Section 1345
sections.original: σὸν ἔργον ὦ πρεσβῦτα φροντίζειν ὅπῃ
sections.translation: It is your business , old man , to consider in what way you shall conquer the man ; for if he were not relying upon something , he would not be so licentious . But he is emboldened by something ; the boldness of the man

sections.label: Section 1350
sections.original: ʼσʼτὶ τὸ λῆμα .
sections.translation: is evident .

sections.label: Section 1351
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐξ ὅτου τὸ πρῶτον ἤρξαθʼ ἡ μάχη γενέσθαι ,
sections.translation: Now you ought to tell to the Chorus from what the contention first arose . And this you must do by all means .

sections.label: Section 1353
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅθεν γε πρῶτον ἠρξάμεσθα λοιδορεῖσθαι
sections.translation: Well , now , I will tell you from what we first began to rail at one another . After we had feasted , as you know ,

sections.label: Section 1355
sections.original: πρῶτον μὲν αὐτὸν τὴν λύραν λαβόντʼ ἐγὼ ʼκέλευσα
sections.translation: I first bade him take a lyre , and sing a song of Simonides , The Shearing of the Ram . But he immediately said it was old-fashioned to play on the lyre and sing while drinking , like a woman grinding parched barley .

sections.label: Section 1359
sections.original: οὐ γὰρ τότʼ εὐθὺς χρῆν σʼ ἄρα τύπτεσθαί τε καὶ πατεῖσθαι ,
sections.translation: For ought you not then immediately to be beaten and trampled on , bidding me sing , just as if you were entertaining cicadae ?

sections.label: Section 1361
sections.original: τοιαῦτα μέντοι καὶ τότʼ ἔλεγεν ἔνδον οἷάπερ νῦν ,
sections.translation: He expressed , however , such opinions then too within , as he does now ; and he asserted that Simonides was a bad poet . I bore it at first , with difficulty indeed , yet nevertheless I bore it . And then I bade him at least take a myrtle-wreath

sections.label: Section 1365
sections.original: τῶν Αἰσχύλου λέξαι τί μοι · κᾆθʼ οὗτος εὐθὺς εἶπεν ·
sections.translation: and recite to me some portion of Aeschylus ; and then he immediately said , Shall I consider Aeschylus the first among the poets , full of empty sound , unpolished , bombastic , using rugged words ? And hereupon you can ’ t think how my heart panted . But , nevertheless , I restrained my passion , and said , At least recite some passage

sections.label: Section 1370
sections.original: λέξον τι τῶν νεωτέρων , ἅττʼ ἐστὶ τὰ σοφὰ ταῦτα .
sections.translation: of the more modern poets , of whatever kind these clever things be . And he immediately sang a passage of Euripides , how a brother , O averter of ill ! Debauched his uterine sister . And I bore it no longer , but immediately assailed him with many abusive reproaches . And then , after that , as was natural ,

sections.label: Section 1375
sections.original: ἔπος πρὸς ἔπος ἠρειδόμεσθʼ · εἶθʼ οὗτος ἐπαναπηδᾶ ,
sections.translation: we hurled word upon word . Then he springs upon me ; and then he was wounding me , and beating me , and throttling me .

sections.label: Section 1377
sections.original: οὔκουν δικαίως , ὅστις οὐκ Εὐριπίδην ἐπαινεῖς
sections.translation: Were you not therefore justly beaten , who do not praise Euripides , the wisest of poets ?

sections.label: Section 1379
sections.original: σοφώτατόν γʼ ἐκεῖνον · ὦ — τί σʼ εἴπω ;
sections.translation: He the wisest ! Oh , what shall I call you ? But I shall be beaten again .

sections.label: Section 1379c
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Δίʼ ἐν δίκῃ γʼ ἄν .
sections.translation: Yes , by Jupiter , with justice .

sections.label: Section 1380
sections.original: καὶ πῶς δικαίως ; ὅστις ὦ ʼναίσχυντέ σʼ ἐξέθρεψα ,
sections.translation: Why , how with justice ? Who , O shameless fellow , reared you , understanding all your wishes , when you lisped what you meant ? If you said bryn , I , understanding it , used to give you to drink . And when you asked for mamman , I used to come to you with bread . And you used no sooner to say caccan , than I used to take and

sections.label: Section 1385
sections.original: ἐξέφερον ἂν καὶ προὐσχόμην σε · σὺ δʼ ἐμὲ νῦν ἀπάγχων
sections.translation: carry you out of doors , and hold you before me .

sections.label: Section 1386
sections.original: βοῶντα καὶ κεκραγόθʼ ὅτι
sections.translation: But you now , throttling me who was bawling and crying out because I wanted to ease myself , had not the heart to carry me forth out of doors , you wretch ; but I did it there while I was being throttled .

sections.label: Section 1391
sections.original: οἶμαί γε τῶν νεωτέρων τὰς καρδίας
sections.translation: I fancy the hearts of the youths are panting to hear what he will say . For if , after having done such things , he shall persuade him by speaking ,

sections.label: Section 1395
sections.original: τὸ δέρμα τῶν γεραιτέρων λάβοιμεν ἂν
sections.translation: I would not take the hide of the old folks , even at the price of a chick-pea .
